summary:
The Qajar Dynasty (1789–1925 AD) was the Turkic tribal confederation that reunified Iran after the fragmentation following [[event:3238|Nader Shah's]] assassination, ruling Persia during a period of intense pressure from expanding European empires — particularly Russia and Britain — and modernization challenges that would eventually lead to the Constitutional Revolution of 1905–1911. The Qajars were founded by Agha Mohammad Khan, a eunuch chieftain who had been castrated as a child hostage and whose brutal ferocity was legendary; he defeated the Zand dynasty, captured and blinded the last Zand ruler Lotf Ali Khan (1794), and established Tehran as the new capital. His assassination in 1797 brought his nephew Fath Ali Shah to the throne.

The Qajar period was defined by Iran's encounter with European imperialism. The Russo-Persian Wars (1804–1813 and 1826–1828) resulted in the Treaties of Gulistan and Turkmenchay, ceding most of the Caucasus (including modern Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Armenia) to Russia — traumatic territorial losses that exposed Qajar military weakness. British and Russian competition for influence in Persia (the 'Great Game') constrained Qajar diplomacy and led to unfavorable trade concessions. Attempts at modernization through European-advised reforms produced reaction and resistance. The dynasty's later decades saw the growth of nationalist movements, tobacco protests (1891–92), and the Constitutional Revolution that forced the establishment of a parliament (Majlis) in 1906 — the beginning of the end of unchecked Qajar authority that would culminate in Reza Shah Pahlavi's coup in 1921.
